title: Allow setting sign-passphrase directly
body:
Signed-off-by: Stephen Reaves <reaves735@gmail.com>

<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

In case you're only changing docs, make sure to prefix the pull-request title with "[CI:DOCS]". That will prevent functional tests from running and save time and energy.

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name. Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

<!--
If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note
is required: Enter your extended release note in the block below. If the PR
requires additional action from users switching to the new release, include the
string "action required".

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

```release-note
Added the '--sign-passphrase' option to allow users to specify gpg signing passphrase directly.
```

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **NOT APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *reavessm* **Once this PR has been reviewed and has the lgtm label**, please assign giuseppe for approval by writing `/assign @giuseppe` in a comment. For more information see:[The Kubernetes Code Review Process](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process). The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman).
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - **[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)** Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

the CI is saying:

```
xref-helpmsgs-manpages: 'podman image push --help' lists '--sign-passphrase', which is not in docs/source/markdown/podman-push.1.md
xref-helpmsgs-manpages: 'podman push --help' lists '--sign-passphrase', which is not in docs/source/markdown/podman-push.1.md
```

But they're clearly [here](https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/15840/files#diff-c7b044b01a2f89b86d880dd61d7525ea6e017f9465eb58e6cac6f4e6f8f288e5R129-R131).  Can I get some help here?  Did I add them in the wrong spot?
You committed a new file, `podman-push.1.md`. You can't do that. Please modify the `.in` file instead.

Thanks @reavessm 
Once this gets merged, could you open a Similar PR for `buildah push`

> Thanks @reavessm Once this gets merged, could you open a Similar PR for `buildah push`

Let's take a step a back and discuss the security concerns first.  Opening an issue ahead of the PR would have helped ironing out design decisions.

Yeah, I’m not happy about making this easy and I’d prefer for this option not to be implemented.

Every user on the system (including “unprivileged” daemons) can see the command lines of other users, revealing the password. Even on a single-user system, that violates the principle of least privilege and exposes the data to way too many accidents. (Erasing in-process secret keys after use was not even protecting data against any recognizable privilege boundary, and was a purely theoretical hassle required by bureaucrats for bad reasons — until Heartbleed happened.)

Stubborn enough users can already submit passphrases without using temporary files, using e.g. process substitution; but this is not exposed to be _easy_ for a reason.

If anything, I’d recommend thinking about making it easy to be more secure, devising a SELinux context for passphrase files so that Podman-only passphrase files can only be read by Podman and not by `unconfined_t`.

@mtrmac @vrothberg  That's a fair concern that I hadn't considered.  As I'm still fighting with these unit tests, I'd be ok with closing this PR.  FWIW though, this doesn't get rid of the more secure option, this only adds a more convenient option.

Almost all users, almost all the time, are going to choose the most convenient option that’s readily available. So I think it’s important for the most convenient option not to be insecure.

There’s certainly a balance to be struck; at _some_ point users are going to actively do work, and share tips, on how to bypass the intended secure design to get a more convenient system. I don’t think we are at that point here — distributing or version-controlling a single script, vs. a script + a passphrase file, seems about equally difficult, but I might be missing some case that needs to be made easier to do securely.
I'd be more comfortable with not exposing this option as well.  I hope this does not discourage you from further contributing to the project, @reavessm!

Would --sign-passphrase-file /dev/stdin work?

Then could you 

echo mysecret | podman push --sign-passphrease-file /dev/stdin ...

We could simplify this to allow

echo mysecret | podman push --sign-passphrease-file - ...

Then translate the `-` to /dev/stdin.

It might be useful to discuss the use case — how is signing being used so that including the passphrase on the command line seems to be the most appropriate option? Is it just some script where it seems to be easier to maintain a single file instead of two? Or something quite different we didn't expect?

> echo mysecret | podman push --sign-passphrease-file /dev/stdin ...

How confident can anyone be that `echo` never becomes `/bin/echo`? And then it’s equivalently insecure.

I’d much rather talk about why this seems attractive in the first place. In _some_ cases (if all of this is really happening under a single UID with no further separation, and no attacks possible in the environment) it might be perfectly fine to have a private key without a passphrase.

In other cases, the key should really be only provided interactively.

And if there is a script … it _seems_ to me like a good idea in any case to separate the very secret configuration data from the mostly-non-secret script. But I might be missing something, and even if I’m not, I appreciate that convincing users to do it this way is a bit of a hurdle.
Based on the conversation I am closing the PR.  Thanks a lot for proposing the change!
